critic_raw:
This RNN versus logistic regression disease severity prediction design makes several well-considered choices but has critical problems in the sequence encoding, baseline construction, and the use of a bidirectional LSTM.

The RNN is a bidirectional LSTM (Bi-LSTM). Bidirectional LSTMs process sequences in both forward and backward directions, meaning the backward pass requires access to future observations relative to any given timestep. For clinical sequence data where the goal is predicting severity at a future point, this is an architectural anachronism: the backward LSTM has access to observations that would not be available at the time of prediction in deployment. In a retrospective dataset where full patient histories are available, the Bi-LSTM will implicitly use future visits to inform its hidden state at earlier timepoints, inflating training performance and creating an irreproducible deployment scenario. The design does not acknowledge this issue.

The sequence is truncated to 24 visits; patients with more than 24 visits have their oldest records dropped ('truncated from oldest records'). For a chronic disease dataset with irregular visit intervals over 5 years, patients with >24 visits are typically the most severely ill or most clinically engaged. Systematically dropping their earliest history may remove foundational diagnostic information (initial disease onset, treatment initiation events) that are most predictive of long-term trajectory. This truncation policy is a silent data selection choice with potential outcome implications.

The logistic regression baseline uses only the most recent severity score as a temporal feature ('only static features — age, sex, diagnosis, Charlson index, and the severity score from the most recent visit'). This is an autoregressive baseline, where the last observed severity score directly predicts the next. For a continuous outcome with temporal autocorrelation (disease severity), the most recent observation is by far the strongest predictor. The Bi-LSTM will need to substantially outperform this simple autoregressive baseline to demonstrate that temporal modeling adds value beyond carry-forward prediction. The design does not discuss this expected performance ceiling.

The inter-rater reliability assessment (target ICC ≥ 0.7) is proposed as a data quality audit, but the decision to 'restrict to high-agreement clinicians' if ICC < 0.7 introduces selection bias: clinicians whose assessments agree more are likely seeing less ambiguous cases, and restricting to high-agreement clinicians systematically excludes the most difficult and most clinically consequential cases from the analysis.
all_issues_raised: The Bi-LSTM uses a backward pass that requires future observations relative to each timestep; when applied to retrospective patient histories, this means the model implicitly uses future visits during training, inflating training performance and creating a deployment scenario that is architecturally unreproducible.

Truncating patient sequences by dropping the oldest records systematically removes the early disease history of the most severely ill patients (who have the most visits), potentially removing diagnostic onset and treatment initiation events that are the strongest predictors of long-term severity trajectory.

The logistic regression baseline includes the most recent severity score as a feature, making it an autoregressive predictor where the last observed value directly predicts the next; this is a strong single-feature baseline that the Bi-LSTM must substantially beat to demonstrate temporal modeling value beyond simple carry-forward prediction.

Restricting to high-agreement clinicians when ICC < 0.7 introduces selection bias by excluding the most ambiguous and clinically complex cases, which are precisely the cases where temporal modeling should provide the most value, making the resulting dataset unrepresentative of the full clinical population.
all_issues_adjudicated: The Bi-LSTM uses a backward pass that requires future observations relative to each timestep; when applied to retrospective patient histories, this means the model implicitly uses future visits during training, inflating training performance and creating a deployment scenario that is architecturally unreproducible.

Truncating patient sequences by dropping the oldest records systematically removes the early disease history of the most severely ill patients (who have the most visits), potentially removing diagnostic onset and treatment initiation events that are the strongest predictors of long-term severity trajectory.

The logistic regression baseline includes the most recent severity score as a feature, making it an autoregressive predictor where the last observed value directly predicts the next; this is a strong single-feature baseline that the Bi-LSTM must substantially beat to demonstrate temporal modeling value beyond simple carry-forward prediction.

Restricting to high-agreement clinicians when ICC < 0.7 introduces selection bias by excluding the most ambiguous and clinically complex cases, which are precisely the cases where temporal modeling should provide the most value, making the resulting dataset unrepresentative of the full clinical population.
